affected_groups: middle-class families invested in current tracks
content.decision_question: Should Hungary pursue full structural detracking (delay selection to age 14) despite its high political, legal, and implementation-disruption costs, or accept that the strongest equity lever is currently politically and legally infeasible?
content.evidence_boundary: Evidence can clarify the magnitude and distribution of segregation effects under early tracking and document transition costs/timelines from comparable reforms (Poland, Finland), but it cannot determine whether the value of reduced segregation outweighs the value of preserving negotiated institutional settlements and maintainer autonomy—that is a normative judgment evidence cannot adjudicate.
content.tension: Removing early selection ages (10/12) most directly targets the mechanism cross-nationally linked to inequality, but doing so requires overriding a symbolically entrenched 1989-90 church-liberal settlement and the highest legal-authority burden of any option, risking severe political backlash and implementation shocks analogous to Poland's 2017 reversal.
content.title: Structural reform versus institutional stability and legal feasibility
